narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, including your plan to attend full-time like most students and a middle-class family income background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 84.9% probability that you would fail it, leading directly to the rejection of your application. This high failure risk means the school sees you as unlikely to succeed on that critical exam right away, a key benchmark they use to ensure incoming students thrive in their program. The decision hinged most heavily on your academic decile rankings from earlier school years, particularly your Year 3 performance, which landed in the bottom decile compared to the average of around the middle of the pack. This low Year 3 standing was the strongest factor pushing the model toward predicting bar failure, as it signals potential challenges in sustaining strong grades over time. Your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom range well below the typical mid-level average, reinforced that concern as the second-biggest influence, suggesting early academic hurdles that carried weight in the prediction. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually pulled in the opposite direction, nudging the model toward expecting bar exam success because it's stronger than what most applicants bring. Compared to typical students, where LSAT scores average 36.7 and yours is lower at 27, those other elements like your deciles overshadowed even your GPA strength and common full-time status. While your background as a Black male doesn't directly factor into the top influences here, the model focused purely on these performance indicators to gauge your bar readiness against patterns from past students. This rejection doesn't define your potential—many overcome early setbacks to excel. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking targeted academic coaching to improve your standing in advanced coursework, and reapplying after building a stronger recent record. Schools value persistence, and addressing these key areas could flip the prediction in your favor next time.
